channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Professor Fired for Criticizing Charlie Kirk Wins $1.9 Million Settlement
item.description: Tamar Shirinian, a former professor at the University of Tennessee at Knoxville, was among those who lost their jobs over their posts about the slain conservative activist.
item.dc:creator: Madaleine Rubin
item.media:credit: Brianna Paciorka/The Knoxville News-Sentinel, via Imagn Images
item.media:description: Tamar Shirinian has reached a $1.9 million settlement with the University of Tennessee at Knoxville.

item.title: Democratic Outsiders Keep Rolling: 5 Takeaways From Colorado’s Primaries
item.description: A democratic socialist ousted a veteran congresswoman in Denver, and a U.S. senator lost his bid for governor. But the state’s other senator fended off a progressive primary challenger.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning
item.media:credit: Chet Strange for The New York Times
item.media:description: Melat Kiros, center, toppled Representative Diana DeGette, a veteran Democrat in Denver.

item.title: Melat Kiros, a Left-Wing Insurgent, Ousts a 15-Term Congresswoman in Colorado
item.description: Melat Kiros, a 29-year-old democratic socialist, unseated Representative Diana DeGette in a Democratic primary to represent the Denver area.
item.dc:creator: Emily Davies
item.media:credit: Chet Strange for The New York Times
item.media:description: Melat Kiros, an immigrant and first-time candidate, is likely to head to Congress in a deep-blue Colorado district.

item.title: Phil Weiser Upsets Michael Bennet in Colorado Democratic Governor Primary
item.description: Phil Weiser beat Senator Michael Bennet in the Democratic primary, presenting himself as the stronger foil for President Trump.
item.dc:creator: Jack Healy
item.media:credit: Cheney Orr for The New York Times
item.media:description: Attorney General Phil Weiser of Colorado speaking with voters during a campaign event at Bluebird Café in Glenwood Springs, Colo.

item.title: Manny Rutinel, a Progressive, Wins Colorado Primary for Competitive House Seat
item.description: Manny Rutinel, a state representative and former activist, will face Representative Gabe Evans, a Republican seen as vulnerable, in the fall.
item.dc:creator: Emily Davies
item.media:credit: Brice Tucker/The Greeley Tribune, via Associated Press
item.media:description: State Representative Manny Rutinel, a progressive, won his primary in Colorado’s most competitive House district. He will face Representative Gabe Evans, a Republican, in the fall.

item.title: Trump’s Moneymaking Run: Unrivaled in Presidential History
item.description: The president’s move to open new business ventures, rather than eliminate potential conflicts, defies a long-held tradition.
item.dc:creator: Eric Lipton
item.media:credit: Pete Marovich for The New York Times
item.media:description: The bulk of the new revenue for President Trump comes from the cryptocurrency industry, which his family aggressively moved into starting in late 2024.

item.title: U.S. Mobilizes for Venezuela Despite Trump’s Disdain for Foreign Aid
item.description: The State Department has promised $100 million in new funds to aid groups, after President Trump was criticized for an anemic response to an earthquake in Myanmar last year.
item.dc:creator: Michael Crowley and Edward Wong
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
United States Agency for International Development
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Volunteers and family members searched for survivors amid several collapsed residential buildings in La Guaira, Venezuela, on Monday. Two powerful earthquakes struck the region last week.

item.title: 10-Year-Old Boy Charged With First-Degree Murder in Shooting of Infant Niece
item.description: The boy shot his 7-month-old niece at a St. Louis home with a firearm that had been kept under a mattress, the police said. The baby’s father, who owned the weapon, has also been charged.
item.dc:creator: John S.W. MacDonald
item.media:credit: James A. Finley/Associated Press
item.media:description: The Mel Carnahan Courthouse in St. Louis, one of the two buildings where the 22nd Judicial Circuit Court of Missouri has offices.

item.title: After a Tap Heard in Rubble, U.S. Rescue Teams Begin a Grueling Marathon
item.description: Surrounded by the devastation of Venezuela’s earthquakes, emergency specialists from California, Virginia and Florida work with locals to search for survivors.
item.dc:creator: Shawn Hubler and Billy Witz
item.media:credit: Matias Delacroix/Associated Press
item.media:description: Members of the Fairfax County Urban Search and Rescue team from Virginia searched for people in the rubble of a collapsed building in La Guaira, Venezuela, on Sunday.

item.title: Trump Pulled in at Least $2.2 Billion in 2025, Financial Disclosure Shows
item.description: The release of a mandatory financial disclosure for 2025 shows that the Trump family’s holdings, particularly the president’s crypto businesses, were stunningly lucrative.
item.dc:creator: Ben Protess, Andrea Fuller, Eric Lipton and David Yaffe-Bellany
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump entering the White House on Sunday. His mandatory financial disclosure report for 2025 pulled back the curtain on his secretive business operations.

item.title: Talarico Seizes on Paxton Tabloid Story to Test Corruption Attacks in Texas Senate Race
item.description: After a salacious report about Ken Paxton, the Republican nominee for Senate in Texas, his Democratic rival, James Talarico, seized on the news — but focused on corruption and affordability.
item.dc:creator: Bayliss Wagner
item.media:credit: Antranik Tavitian for The New York Times; Callaghan O'Hare for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ken Paxton and James Talarico are locked in a competitive race for Senate in Texas.

item.title: Trump Announces Republican Midterm Convention in Dallas, Texas
item.description: Parties normally hold conventions only in presidential years, but President Trump liked the idea of mobilizing Republicans this fall.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher
item.media:credit: Desiree Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Dallas will be the site of Republican efforts to mobilize ahead of the Nov. 3 midterm elections.

item.title: Takeaways From a Transformative Supreme Court Term
item.description: The justices rejected some of President Trump’s signature initiatives, but delivered lasting, long-sought conservative wins.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow and Adam Liptak
item.media:credit: Allison Robbert for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Supreme Court on Tuesday said states were free to bar transgender girls from competing in girls’ and women’s school sports.

item.title: 4 Warning Signs for Graham Platner. And 4 for Susan Collins Too.
item.description: The Maine race, which both parties see as key to winning control of the Senate, is extremely close, a Times/Portland Press Herald/Siena poll found. Both candidates have reasons for concern.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times; Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Graham Platner and Susan Collins

item.title: C.I.A. Reorganization Prioritizes Cyberoperations
item.description: John Ratcliffe, the C.I.A. director, said that the agency would take “smart risks,” but that people would have oversight of artificial intelligence.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: John Ratcliffe, the C.I.A. director, said the overhaul was an acknowledgment that in the modern world, digital borders are as important as physical borders.

item.title: States Sue Trump Administration Over Medicaid Work Requirements
item.description: The lawsuit argues that new federal rules went beyond what Congress enacted and broke from guidance that the federal government previewed to states.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Kliff and Margot Sanger-Katz
item.category: Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: The Trump administration is being sued on the new Medicaid work requirements, arguing that the guidelines are inconsistent with federal law.

item.title: Bear Breaks Into Truck, Helping Itself to Handyman’s Lunch
item.description: A handyman was returning to his truck when he opened the door and found a cub eating his lunch from the passenger seat in Steamboat Springs, Colo.
item.dc:creator: Johnny Diaz

item.title: Trans Sports Ruling Puts Pressure on States Without Bans
item.description: Almost half of states do not prohibit trans girls and women from competing. The Supreme Court ruling doesn’t force them to, but lawmakers and voters could change that.
item.dc:creator: Amy Harmon
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Supreme Court in Washington on Tuesday.

item.title: 55% of Americans support birthright citizenship.
item.description: The Supreme Court decision puts the court in line with the views of a majority of Americans.
item.dc:creator: Ruth Igielnik
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times

item.title: G.O.P. Revolt Paralyzes the House, Stalling Trump Agenda
item.description: A Republican blockade derailed a Pentagon policy bill and other legislation as the far right pressed for action on a voting bill championed by President Trump.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison, Carl Hulse and Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Speaker Mike Johnson at the Capitol on Tuesday.

item.title: Amy Coney Barrett Faces Right-Wing Ire Over Recent Rulings
item.description: Justice Barrett sided with Chief Justice Roberts and the liberal minority to uphold birthright citizenship and mail-in voting practices.
item.dc:creator: Emily Davies
item.media:credit: Elizabeth Frantz/Reuters
item.media:description: Supreme Court Justice Amy Coney Barrett has become a target of the right for voting with liberal justices on several cases this year.

item.title: Trump Says It’s ‘Too Bad’ Supreme Court Upheld Birthright Citizenship
item.description: The court’s decision was a major rebuke to President Trump, who falsely asserted that Congress could reverse it through legislation.
item.dc:creator: Erica L. Green and Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross for The New York Times
item.media:description: If President Trump were to pressure Congress to take up birthright legislation, it would be an exceedingly difficult vote ahead of the midterm elections.

item.title: Woman Wading in Central Florida River Is Fatally Bitten by Alligator
item.description: The woman, 31, was kneeling in the shallow part of a river in Seminole County, in Central Florida, when an alligator bit her, the authorities said.
item.dc:creator: Christine Hauser
item.category: Florida Fish and Wildlife Conservation Commission
item.media:credit: Scott McIntyre for The New York Times
item.media:description: Alligators have been “very active and highly territorial” in Florida as the mating season comes to an end, a state wildlife officer said.

item.title: Supreme Court to Weigh Constitutional Protection for AR-15 Rifles
item.description: Two cases to be heard in the fall challenge legality of state and local bans on the weapons, which are popular with gun owners and have been used in mass shootings.
item.dc:creator: Mattathias Schwartz and Ann E. Marimow
item.category: New York State Rifle & Pistol Association v Bruen (Supreme Court Decision)
New York State Rifle and Pistol Assn
item.media:credit: Paul Ratje for The New York Times
item.media:description: Custom-painted AR-15 rifles at a gun show in Phoenix in January.

item.title: What the Supreme Court Decisions About Trump’s Firings Mean
item.description: Our chief legal affairs correspondent, Adam Liptak, explains how two Supreme Court rulings on the firings of independent regulators first expand the power of the president, and then carve out an exception.
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak, Nikolay Nikolov, Gilad Thaler, Nour Idriss, Rafaela Balster, Stephanie Swart and Ray Whitehouse

item.title: What to Know About the Supreme Court’s Birthright Citizenship Order
item.description: The decision ends one of the most aggressive parts of Trump’s immigration agenda. But hundreds of other restrictions have taken effect.
item.dc:creator: Jazmine Ulloa
item.media:credit: Amir Hamja/The New York Times
item.media:description: The Supreme Court ruled against the president 6 to 3.

item.title: Supreme Court Ruling on Trans Athletes Leaves Advocates Crestfallen
item.description: The ruling upholding two state laws blocking transgender athletes from participating in girls’ and women’s sports was the latest in a series of defeats.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow, Juliet Macur and Amy Harmon
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Transgender pride flags outside the Supreme Court in Washington in January.

item.title: In dissents, justices decried the ruling as a ‘serious mistake’ that would aid ‘birth tourists.’
item.dc:creator: Mattathias Schwartz
item.media:credit: Erin Schaff/The New York Times

item.title: The Supreme Court Just Gave the G.O.P. a New Midterm Edge
item.description: The decision, which allows parties to spend more in coordination with candidates, is likely to further expand the power of big money in American politics.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher
item.category: Citizens United v Federal Election Commission (Supreme Court Decision)
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Supreme Court’s ruling on party spending restrictions was expected to give Republicans a significant financial advantage in this year’s midterms.

item.title: The 1952 Law That Was Central to the Birthright Citizenship Case
item.description: When Congress passed a sweeping immigration reform measure after World War II, it included language that mirrored the 14th Amendment’s citizenship clause — and perhaps even took it a step further.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.media:credit: Ernst Haas/Getty Images
item.media:description: A ship arriving at Ellis Island in 1951.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle

item.title: Tom Kean, New Jersey Congressman, Says Depression Led to Long Absence
item.description: After months of mystery, the New Jersey representative broke his silence about the undisclosed health issue that prompted his 117-day absence.
item.dc:creator: Annie Karni
item.media:credit: Alex Kent/The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Thomas H. Kean Jr., a New Jersey Republican, at the Capitol on Tuesday.

item.title: Plaintiff in Supreme Court Transgender Athlete Case Recently Won a State Shot Put Title
item.description: The success of Becky Pepper-Jackson, a West Virginia high school sophomore, prompted criticism from the state’s governor.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Becky Pepper-Jackson also placed fourth in discus throw at the West Virginia state track and field championships last month.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow

item.title: Supreme Court Upholds Birthright Citizenship, Blocking a Key Trump Policy
item.description: The justices blocked President Trump’s executive order that banned birthright citizenship for the children of undocumented immigrants and some temporary foreign visitors.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Demonstrators gathered at the Supreme Court in support of birthright citizenship in April.

item.title: Supreme Court Lifts Spending Limits on Political Parties and Candidates
item.description: Republicans had asked the court to strike down restrictions on how much political parties can spend in coordination with candidates.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle and Adam Liptak
item.category: Citizens United v Federal Election Commission (Supreme Court Decision)
item.media:credit: Jon Cherry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Delegates and supporters at the Republican National Convention in 2024. The National Republican Senatorial Committee brought a legal challenge against limits on coordinated spending when JD Vance ran for Senate in 2022.

item.title: Supreme Court Allows States to Bar Transgender Athletes From Girls’ Sports
item.description: The court’s decision involving laws from West Virginia and Idaho has implications for 25 other states with similar restrictions on transgender female athletes joining women’s sports teams.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Becky Pepper-Jackson challenged West Virginia’s laws limiting transgender athletes from competing in women’s sports.

item.title: 2 Killed as Bus Flips Into Traffic on Long Island Expressway
item.description: A bus crashed into multiple vehicles in Queens just before midnight on Monday. Authorities investigating the incident have closed lanes on the highway near 50th Street.
item.dc:creator: Isabella Kwai

item.title: Times/Siena Poll Finds Talarico and Paxton Tied in Texas Senate Race
item.description: James Talarico, a Democratic state lawmaker, is tied with Ken Paxton, the Republican state attorney general, according to a New York Times/Siena poll.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher, Ruth Igielnik and Camille Baker
item.media:credit: Edison Wu/The New York Times

item.title: The Founding Father Who Sought a Last-Minute Deal to Avert the Revolution
item.description: Recently discovered documents reveal a back-channel attempt by a leader of the Continental Congress to make peace. (He did not succeed.)
item.dc:creator: Sarah Lyall
item.media:credit: Lauren O'Neil for The New York Times
item.media:description: Letters written by John Dickinson, a member of the Continental Congress, displayed in Cambridge, Mass.
